summary: O'Neil reflects on her 25+ years of volunteer work with Fair Foods, a food rescue program in Dorchester, Massachusetts. She contrasts the crack epidemic era of the late 1980s with today's quieter but equally devastating poverty crisis. The lines for salvaged food have grown longer, neighborhoods show signs of economic decay, and many children she once knew are now incarcerated. She argues that despite lower crime rates, we've created a different kind of hell—one of quiet desperation and growing hunger, made more unjust by simultaneous food stamp cuts and Wall Street bonuses.
key_points: Fair Foods rescues produce too old for supermarkets but still edible, distributing it at 'dollar bag' sites to feed low-income families
Demand for salvaged food has increased dramatically over 25 years, despite the end of the crack epidemic and lower crime rates
The visible markers of neighborhood decline—abandoned Victorians, fewer cars, incarcerated youth—tell a story of quiet economic devastation
The juxtaposition of food stamp cuts with banker bonuses reveals a profound moral failure in American priorities
money_quotes: But nowadays, when the crime rate is so much lower, we're seeing another kind of hell. It's a lot quieter.
It's incredibly sad to see how much more demand there is for salvaged food now than there was 25 years ago.
Many of the customers of Fair Foods are old friends by now, they've been coming weekly to various sites for many years to feed their children and their grandchildren.
It makes me wonder what kind of country we've created where people are so hungry, we're reducing food stamps, and Jamie Dimon is getting an extra big bonus.
Where is the justice in that?
key_insight: The decline in visible crime has masked a quieter, deeper crisis of poverty and hunger that reveals the moral bankruptcy of a system that rewards bankers while cutting food assistance.
